meta.title: gam

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: gam
root.mentor_note: gam quietly counts: one marriage, two, or many.

attempt.direction_prompt: Married to one person, or to several at once?

context_sentences: The species was once assumed to practise strict monogamy, until researchers tracked the pairs more closely.
Monogamy was the legal norm throughout the region, whatever older customs said otherwise.
He was eventually charged with bigamy after both marriage certificates surfaced in the same file.
Bigamy carried a heavier sentence in that country than in almost anywhere else in the region.
Polygamy remained legal there under religious law, though civil law recognised only one spouse.
The census had no simple category for households shaped by polygamy, and researchers adapted their own.

construct_options.text: The practice of having more than one spouse at the same time
A wedding celebrated by many guests at once
The practice of never marrying at all

context_sentences: A committed misogamist, he had turned down three proposals before his friends stopped asking why.
The character reads at first as a misogamist, though the play slowly reveals a much older heartbreak behind it.

construct_options.text: Someone who hates or strongly opposes the institution of marriage
Someone who has been married many times
Someone who hates or distrusts other people generally
